articles.headline: Supreme Court Stays UGC Equity Regulations, 2026 Amidst Misuse Concerns
articles.explainer:
• The Supreme Court of India today issued an interim stay on the implementation of the University Grants Commission (UGC) Equity Regulations, 2026.
• The Court cited concerns regarding the potential for misuse of provisions by 'specific groups', following several petitions challenging the regulations' framework.
• The regulations aimed to promote equitable access and representation in higher education institutions, potentially through affirmative action policies or specific funding allocations.
• This decision is expected to have significant implications for admissions processes, faculty recruitment, and institutional autonomy across universities and colleges nationwide.
• The apex court has sought detailed responses from the UGC and the Ministry of Education, scheduling the next hearing in April.
gsSubTopics: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure.
Functions and responsibilities of the Union and the States, issues and challenges pertaining to the federal structure, devolution of powers and finances up to local levels and challenges therein.
Structure, organization and functioning of the Executive and the Judiciary Ministries and Departments of the Government; pressure groups and formal/informal associations and their role in the Polity.
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
prelimsPoints: Supreme Court's power of judicial review (Article 13, 32, 226).
Role and functions of University Grants Commission (UGC) as a statutory body.
Constitutional provisions related to equality and affirmative action (Articles 14, 15, 16).
Difference between interim stay and permanent injunction.
mainsPoints: Examine the balance between legislative intent (UGC Regulations) and judicial oversight (SC stay) in policy implementation.
Discuss the constitutional validity and socio-economic implications of affirmative action policies in higher education.
Analyze the challenges of implementing equity regulations and preventing their misuse, considering diverse societal interests.
Evaluate the role of the judiciary in safeguarding fundamental rights and ensuring fair implementation of government policies.
articles.imageDescription: Illustration of the Supreme Court of India building with legal documents in the foreground.
articles.trivia: The first UGC was established in 1945 to oversee the three Central Universities of Aligarh, Banaras, and Delhi, and its scope was extended in 1947 to cover all Indian universities.

articles.headline: India-Japan 10th Strategic Dialogue Concludes, Focus on Indo-Pacific Security and Economic Cooperation
articles.explainer:
• The 10th India-Japan Strategic Dialogue concluded in New Delhi, reaffirming commitment to a 'Special Strategic and Global Partnership'.
• Discussions focused on enhancing cooperation in the Indo-Pacific region, counter-terrorism, and defense exchanges.
• Both nations emphasized strengthening supply chain resilience and accelerating investments in infrastructure and digital technologies.
• Key agreements were reached on joint projects in renewable energy and critical minerals extraction, leveraging Japan's technology and India's growing market.
• The dialogue also addressed regional challenges, including maritime security and stability in East Asia.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighborhood- relations.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
prelimsPoints: Key pillars of India-Japan relations (security, economy, technology).
Significance of the Indo-Pacific region for both countries.
Role of QUAD grouping and other multilateral forums involving India and Japan.
Major economic and strategic projects between India and Japan (e.g., bullet train, infrastructure).
mainsPoints: Analyze the evolving strategic partnership between India and Japan in the context of changing geopolitical dynamics in the Indo-Pacific.
Discuss how economic cooperation, particularly in critical sectors like renewable energy and digital tech, strengthens India's strategic autonomy.
Evaluate the challenges and opportunities for India-Japan collaboration in maintaining regional security and stability.
Examine the role of bilateral dialogues in shaping India's foreign policy objectives and addressing global challenges.
articles.imageDescription: Flags of India and Japan displayed during a diplomatic meeting.
articles.trivia: Japan is one of the few countries with which India has a 2+2 Ministerial Dialogue (Foreign and Defence Ministers).

articles.headline: Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission Expands to Cover All Primary Health Centers by Year-End
articles.explainer:
• The Union Ministry of Health and Family Welfare announced an ambitious plan to integrate all Primary Health Centers (PHCs) and Community Health Centers (CHCs) into the Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission (ABDM) by the end of 2026.
• This expansion aims to create a seamless digital health ecosystem, enabling electronic health records (EHRs) for all citizens and improving access to healthcare services.
• Training programs for healthcare workers in rural areas will be scaled up to ensure effective adoption of digital tools and platforms.
• The initiative is expected to enhance data-driven policymaking, reduce healthcare disparities, and streamline patient management across public health facilities.
• Challenges related to digital literacy, internet connectivity in remote areas, and data privacy were acknowledged, with mitigation strategies being developed.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.

Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes; mechanisms, laws, institutions and Bodies constituted for the protection and betterment of these vulnerable sections.
prelimsPoints: Objectives and components of Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission.
Role of PHCs and CHCs in India's healthcare delivery system.
Significance of Health ID and its features.
Challenges in digitalizing healthcare in India (connectivity, literacy, privacy).
mainsPoints: Discuss the potential of the Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission to revolutionize healthcare delivery in India, particularly in rural areas.
Analyze the socio-economic benefits and challenges associated with the widespread adoption of digital health records.
Critically evaluate the government's strategy for expanding ABDM, considering issues of data privacy, digital divide, and infrastructure.
Suggest measures to ensure inclusive and equitable access to digital healthcare services for all sections of society.
articles.imageDescription: A graphic depicting the flow of digital health records between various healthcare providers and patients.
articles.trivia: ABDM aims to create a seamless online platform through the provision of a wide range of data, information and infrastructure services, leveraging open, interoperable, standards-based digital systems.

articles.headline: ISRO Prepares for Mars Orbiter Mission-2 Launch by End of 2026
articles.explainer:
• The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) confirmed its readiness to launch the Mars Orbiter Mission-2 (MOM-2), also known as Mangalyaan-2, by the end of 2026.
• MOM-2 aims for a more advanced study of the Martian surface, atmosphere, and subsurface, carrying a suite of new scientific instruments.
• This mission builds upon the success of Mangalyaan-1, which made India the first Asian nation to reach Mars orbit and the first country to do so in its maiden attempt.
• Key objectives include understanding the evolution of Mars, searching for signs of past or present life, and analyzing Martian geology and mineralogy.
• The mission will likely involve an orbiter, and potentially a lander/rover component, though specifics are still under wraps.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
Awareness in the fields of Space.
prelimsPoints: First Mars mission (MOM-1) and its achievements.
Objectives of MOM-2 (Mangalyaan-2).
Scientific instruments typically carried on planetary missions.
Key aspects of Mars' environment (atmosphere, geology, water history).
Launch vehicles used by ISRO for deep space missions (e.g., PSLV, GSLV).
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of India's Mars missions for its space program, scientific community, and international standing.
Analyze the scientific objectives of MOM-2 and how they contribute to our understanding of planetary evolution and habitability.
Evaluate the technological challenges involved in interplanetary missions and ISRO's capabilities in overcoming them.
Examine the broader implications of space exploration for national development, technological innovation, and international cooperation.
articles.imageDescription: Artistic rendition of an Indian satellite orbiting Mars with its scientific instruments deployed.
articles.trivia: India's Mangalyaan (MOM-1) mission was the cheapest Mars mission ever, costing only about $74 million.

articles.headline: GST Collections Hit Record High in January 2026, Signifying Robust Economic Growth
articles.explainer:
• Goods and Services Tax (GST) collections for January 2026 (for transactions in December 2025) reached an all-time high, surpassing previous records.
• The Ministry of Finance attributed the surge to sustained economic recovery, increased compliance, and festive demand.
• This robust collection indicates a healthy growth trajectory for the Indian economy, reflecting strong manufacturing and service sector activity.
• The higher tax revenue provides the government with greater fiscal space for infrastructure spending and social welfare programs.
• Experts suggest that improved tax administration and technology-driven compliance measures have also played a crucial role in enhancing collections.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
Government Budgeting.
Inclusive growth and issues arising from it.
prelimsPoints: Components of GST (CGST, SGST, IGST, UTGST).
Role of GST Council and its composition.
Impact of GST on Indian economy (pros and cons).
Relationship between tax collection and economic indicators (GDP growth, consumption).
mainsPoints: Analyze the factors contributing to the record GST collections and their implications for India's fiscal health and economic stability.
Discuss how a robust tax collection system can support government initiatives for infrastructure development and social welfare.
Evaluate the effectiveness of GST in streamlining indirect taxation and promoting ease of doing business in India.
Examine the challenges that remain in further improving GST compliance and expanding the tax base.
articles.imageDescription: A bar graph showing increasing GST collection trends over months.
articles.trivia: GST was implemented in India on 1 July 2017, replacing multiple cascading taxes levied by the central and state governments.

articles.headline: New National Policy on Climate-Resilient Agriculture Launched to Bolster Food Security
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Agriculture and Farmers' Welfare unveiled a comprehensive National Policy on Climate-Resilient Agriculture (NPCRA).
• The policy aims to integrate climate change adaptation and mitigation strategies into agricultural practices across the country.
• Key focus areas include promoting drought-resistant crop varieties, efficient water management techniques, and sustainable land use practices.
• NPCRA will facilitate research and development in climate-smart agriculture, provide financial incentives to farmers, and enhance capacity building.
• The initiative is crucial for ensuring food security and farmers' livelihoods in the face of increasing climate variability and extreme weather events.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Disaster and disaster management.

Major crops cropping patterns in various parts of the country, different types of irrigation and irrigation systems storage, transport and marketing of agricultural produce and issues and related constraints; e-technology in the aid of farmers.

Issues related to direct and indirect farm subsidies and minimum support prices; Public Distribution System objectives, functioning, limitations, revamping; issues of buffer stocks and food security; Technology missions; economics of animal-rearing.
prelimsPoints: Definition of climate-resilient agriculture.
Key components of the new NPCRA policy.
Examples of climate-smart agricultural practices.
Government schemes related to agricultural sustainability (e.g., PM Fasal Bima Yojana, Paramparagat Krishi Vikas Yojana).
mainsPoints: Discuss the imperative of adopting climate-resilient agricultural practices in India to safeguard food security and farmers' income.
Analyze the key features and potential impact of the National Policy on Climate-Resilient Agriculture on Indian farming.
Evaluate the challenges in implementing such a policy, including technological adoption, farmer awareness, and financial support.
Suggest a multi-stakeholder approach for promoting sustainable and climate-resilient agriculture across different agro-climatic zones in India.
articles.imageDescription: A farmer working in a field with drought-resistant crops under a bright sun.
articles.trivia: India is highly vulnerable to climate change due to its large population dependent on agriculture and its diverse agro-climatic zones.

articles.headline: India Initiates Further Review of Indus Water Treaty Provisions Amidst Persistent Disputes
articles.explainer:
• India has commenced a detailed review of certain provisions of the Indus Water Treaty (IWT) with Pakistan, citing concerns over Pakistan's objections to hydroelectric projects.
• The review aims to explore mechanisms for optimal utilization of India's share of water from the Western Rivers, as per the treaty's mandate.
• This move comes after several rounds of failed bilateral talks and arbitration efforts at international forums regarding projects like Kishenganga and Ratle.
• India reiterates its commitment to the IWT but emphasizes the need for a 'fair and equitable' interpretation that recognizes its developmental needs.
• Experts suggest this could lead to increased pressure on Pakistan to engage constructively or escalate diplomatic tensions.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighborhood- relations.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
prelimsPoints: Year of signing and key provisions of the Indus Water Treaty.
Which rivers are allocated to India and Pakistan under IWT.
Role of the Permanent Indus Commission.
Key hydroelectric projects causing disputes (Kishenganga, Ratle).
mainsPoints: Discuss the historical context and strategic importance of the Indus Water Treaty for both India and Pakistan.
Analyze the reasons behind India's decision to review IWT provisions and its potential implications for bilateral relations.
Evaluate the challenges and opportunities for peaceful resolution of water disputes between riparian states, citing international best practices.
Examine how water resources can be a source of both conflict and cooperation in South Asia.
articles.imageDescription: Map showing the Indus River System and the allocation of rivers under the IWT.
articles.trivia: The Indus Waters Treaty was brokered by the World Bank and signed in 1960 by Prime Minister Jawaharlal Nehru and President Ayub Khan.

articles.headline: Government Unveils New National Cyber Security Framework 2.0 to Combat Evolving Threats
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY) launched the National Cyber Security Framework 2.0, updating the previous policy from 2013.
• The new framework emphasizes a proactive and adaptive approach to cyber defense, focusing on critical infrastructure protection, threat intelligence sharing, and public-private partnerships.
• It introduces stricter data governance norms, mandatory incident reporting for critical sectors, and enhanced training for cybersecurity professionals.
• The policy aims to foster indigenous cybersecurity capabilities and promote research and development in emerging technologies like AI and quantum computing for security applications.
• This move is crucial for safeguarding India's digital economy and protecting citizens from increasingly sophisticated cyberattacks.
gsSubTopics: Challenges to internal security through communication networks, role of media and social networking sites in internal security challenges, basics of cyber security; money-laundering and its prevention.
Security challenges and their management in border areas; linkages of organized crime with terrorism.
prelimsPoints: Key objectives of National Cyber Security Framework 2.0.
Role of CERT-In (Indian Computer Emergency Response Team).
Concept of Critical Information Infrastructure (CII).
Types of cyber threats (ransomware, phishing, DDoS).
Differences from previous cybersecurity policies.
mainsPoints: Discuss the evolving landscape of cyber threats and the necessity for India to adopt a robust and adaptive national cybersecurity framework.
Analyze the key provisions of the National Cyber Security Framework 2.0 and its potential impact on critical sectors and data protection.
Evaluate the challenges in implementing a comprehensive cybersecurity policy, including technological gaps, human resource shortages, and international cooperation.
Suggest measures to strengthen India's cyber resilience, emphasizing public awareness, private sector involvement, and indigenous innovation.
articles.imageDescription: A stylized digital network grid with a lock icon, representing cybersecurity.
articles.trivia: India ranks among the top countries globally in terms of internet users, making it a significant target for cyberattacks.

articles.headline: Namami Gange Mission Enters Phase III, Focus on Tributaries and Sustainable River Basin Management
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Jal Shakti announced the commencement of Phase III of the Namami Gange Mission, expanding its scope beyond the main stem of the Ganga.
• This new phase will primarily focus on rejuvenating the tributaries of the Ganga, including Yamuna, Ramganga, and Gomti, addressing pollution hotspots.
• Emphasis will be laid on decentralized wastewater treatment, promoting circular economy principles, and community participation in river conservation.
• Integrated river basin management plans will be developed for all major tributaries to ensure holistic ecological restoration.
• The initiative aims to achieve 'Nirmal' (clean) and 'Aviral' (uninterrupted flow) Ganga, along with its entire river system.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Disaster and disaster management.
prelimsPoints: Objectives of Namami Gange Mission.
Key focus areas of Phase III (tributaries).
Major tributaries of the Ganga river system.
Concept of 'Nirmal Ganga' and 'Aviral Ganga'.
Types of pollution affecting river systems.
mainsPoints: Discuss the evolution and achievements of the Namami Gange Mission and the necessity of extending its focus to tributaries in Phase III.
Analyze the challenges involved in comprehensive river basin management, considering diverse sources of pollution and stakeholder interests.
Evaluate the role of decentralized wastewater treatment and community participation in achieving sustainable river rejuvenation.
Suggest innovative approaches and policy reforms to enhance the effectiveness of river conservation efforts in India.
articles.imageDescription: A graphic showing the Ganga river system with its major tributaries highlighted.
articles.trivia: The Ganga river basin is the largest in India, covering over 25% of the country's land area and supporting about 40% of its population.

articles.headline: Government Proposes Further Amendments to E-Waste Management Rules, 2022 for Extended Producer Responsibility
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change proposed new amendments to the E-Waste Management Rules, 2022.
• The amendments aim to strengthen the Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) framework, making manufacturers more accountable for the entire lifecycle of electronic products.
• Key changes include revised collection targets, stricter penalties for non-compliance, and mandates for the use of refurbished parts in new products.
• The move is intended to address the rapidly growing volume of electronic waste, promote a circular economy, and reduce environmental impact.
• Stakeholder consultations are underway to finalize the updated rules, with a focus on ease of doing business while enhancing environmental protection.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
prelimsPoints: Definition and sources of e-waste.
Concept of Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR).
Key provisions of E-Waste Management Rules, 2022.
Environmental and health hazards of improper e-waste disposal.
Principles of circular economy.
mainsPoints: Discuss the growing challenge of e-waste in India and the necessity of robust regulatory frameworks like the E-Waste Management Rules.
Analyze how strengthening the Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) mechanism can foster a circular economy and sustainable consumption patterns.
Evaluate the effectiveness of current e-waste management practices and the potential impact of the proposed amendments.
Suggest policy interventions and technological solutions to enhance e-waste collection, recycling, and safe disposal in India.
articles.imageDescription: A pile of discarded electronic gadgets symbolizing e-waste.
articles.trivia: India is one of the top five e-waste generating countries in the world.

articles.headline: India-Africa Free Trade Agreement Negotiations Advance, Boosting South-South Cooperation
articles.explainer:
• India and a bloc of African nations have made significant progress in negotiations for a comprehensive Free Trade Agreement (FTA).
• The proposed FTA aims to reduce tariffs, streamline customs procedures, and enhance market access for goods and services between India and participating African countries.
• This initiative is part of India's broader strategy to strengthen South-South cooperation and diversify its trade partnerships.
• Key sectors targeted for increased trade and investment include pharmaceuticals, automotive, agriculture, and digital services.
• The agreement is expected to foster economic growth, create employment opportunities, and build stronger diplomatic ties.
gsSubTopics: Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
prelimsPoints: Definition and objectives of a Free Trade Agreement (FTA).
Key sectors of India-Africa trade.
Concept of South-South Cooperation.
India's existing trade agreements with other regions/countries.
mainsPoints: Discuss the strategic importance of an India-Africa Free Trade Agreement in strengthening economic ties and promoting South-South cooperation.
Analyze the potential benefits and challenges for India and African nations in implementing a comprehensive FTA.
Evaluate how such agreements contribute to India's foreign policy objectives and its role as a global economic power.
Examine the impact of trade liberalization on developing economies, considering issues of industrial development and local livelihoods.
articles.imageDescription: Flags of India and various African nations converging, symbolizing trade partnership.
articles.trivia: India is Africa's fourth-largest trading partner, and bilateral trade has been growing steadily over the past two decades.

articles.headline: India's First Private Satellite Constellation 'BharatNet' Achieves Full Operational Capability
articles.explainer:
• 'BharatNet', India's first privately developed satellite constellation for broadband internet, announced achieving full operational capability today.
• The constellation, comprising 50 low-Earth orbit (LEO) satellites, aims to provide high-speed, low-latency internet connectivity across rural and remote areas of India.
• This milestone marks a significant step in democratizing internet access and bridging the digital divide, complementing existing terrestrial networks.
• The project, developed by an Indian space tech startup, received substantial government support through the new Space Policy 2025.
• 'BharatNet' is expected to foster innovation in digital services, e-governance, and telemedicine, especially in underserved regions.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
Awareness in the fields of Space.
Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
prelimsPoints: Concept of satellite constellations and LEO satellites.
Benefits of satellite broadband for remote areas.
Government's new Space Policy 2025 and private sector participation.
Role of private companies in India's space sector.
Difference between LEO, MEO, and GEO satellites.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of private sector participation in India's space industry, particularly for initiatives like satellite constellations.
Analyze how 'BharatNet' can contribute to bridging the digital divide and promoting inclusive growth in India's rural and remote areas.
Evaluate the economic and strategic implications of developing indigenous satellite broadband capabilities for national security and technological sovereignty.
Examine the challenges and opportunities for private space startups in India, considering regulatory frameworks and investment climate.
articles.imageDescription: An artist's impression of multiple small satellites orbiting Earth, connected by light beams.
articles.trivia: The term 'BharatNet' is also used for a government project to provide broadband connectivity to gram panchayats using optical fiber.

articles.headline: New National Heritage Conservation Policy to Protect Intangible Cultural Heritage
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Culture launched the 'National Policy for the Conservation of Intangible Cultural Heritage (ICH)', a first of its kind in India.
• The policy aims to identify, document, preserve, and promote India's rich intangible cultural heritage, including oral traditions, performing arts, social practices, rituals, festive events, and traditional craftsmanship.
• It will establish a national registry for ICH, provide funding for community-led conservation projects, and integrate ICH into educational curricula.
• The initiative seeks to empower local communities and traditional practitioners who are custodians of this heritage, ensuring its transmission to future generations.
• This policy is crucial for safeguarding India's diverse cultural identity and promoting cultural tourism.
gsSubTopics: Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature and Architecture from ancient to modern times.
Salient features of Indian Society, Diversity of India.
prelimsPoints: Definition of Intangible Cultural Heritage (ICH).
Examples of ICH recognized by UNESCO from India (e.g., Yoga, Kumbh Mela).
Key features of the new National Policy.
Role of Ministry of Culture.
Difference between tangible and intangible heritage.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of India's intangible cultural heritage and the necessity of a dedicated policy for its conservation and promotion.
Analyze the challenges involved in identifying, documenting, and safeguarding ICH, particularly in a diverse country like India.
Evaluate how community participation and integration into education can ensure the vitality and transmission of traditional knowledge and practices.
Examine the potential of ICH for cultural tourism and its role in strengthening national identity and international cultural diplomacy.
articles.imageDescription: An image depicting various forms of Indian performing arts and traditional crafts.
articles.trivia: UNESCO's 2003 Convention for the Safeguarding of the Intangible Cultural Heritage aims to protect such treasures worldwide.

articles.headline: National Urban Flood Resilience Mission Launched to Tackle Increasing Urban Flooding
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs launched the 'National Urban Flood Resilience Mission' (NUFRM) to address the growing challenge of urban flooding across Indian cities.
• The mission adopts an integrated approach, focusing on improved drainage infrastructure, early warning systems, sponge city concepts, and community preparedness.
• It will provide financial and technical assistance to states and urban local bodies for developing and implementing city-specific flood management plans.
• NUFRM emphasizes the use of geospatial technology and data analytics for flood mapping and risk assessment.
• This timely intervention aims to minimize economic losses, protect lives, and enhance urban liveability in the face of extreme weather events.
gsSubTopics: Disaster and disaster management.
Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
prelimsPoints: Causes of urban flooding in India.
Concept of 'Sponge City' and its features.
Role of National Disaster Management Authority (NDMA).
Components of early warning systems for floods.
Impact of climate change on urban extreme weather events.
mainsPoints: Discuss the increasing vulnerability of Indian cities to urban flooding and the multi-faceted challenges it poses to urban infrastructure and livelihoods.
Analyze the key features of the National Urban Flood Resilience Mission and how it aims to address these challenges through an integrated approach.
Evaluate the importance of adopting 'Sponge City' principles and leveraging technology for effective urban flood management.
Suggest measures to enhance community participation and inter-agency coordination for building flood-resilient cities in India.
articles.imageDescription: An aerial view of a city street partially submerged in floodwater.
articles.trivia: Mumbai, Chennai, and Bengaluru are some of the major Indian cities frequently affected by urban flooding.

articles.headline: Government Announces Enhanced Incentives for Advanced Semiconductor Manufacturing in India
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology announced a significant enhancement of its Production Linked Incentive (PLI) scheme for semiconductor and display manufacturing.
• The revised incentives aim to attract more global chipmakers and foster a robust indigenous semiconductor ecosystem in India.
• Key provisions include higher fiscal support for capital expenditure, R&D grants, and preferential market access for domestically produced chips.
• This move is critical for reducing India's reliance on imported semiconductors, boosting electronics manufacturing, and achieving technological self-reliance.
• The policy is expected to create thousands of high-skilled jobs and position India as a key player in the global semiconductor supply chain.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
Effects of liberalization on the economy, changes in industrial policy and their effects on industrial growth.
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life; indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
prelimsPoints: Importance of semiconductors in modern economy.
Objectives of Production Linked Incentive (PLI) schemes.
Government initiatives to boost electronics manufacturing (e.g., Make in India).
Challenges in establishing semiconductor fabs in India (high capital, technology transfer).
Types of semiconductors (memory, logic, analog).
mainsPoints: Discuss the strategic importance of developing an indigenous semiconductor manufacturing ecosystem for India's economic growth and national security.
Analyze how enhanced PLI schemes and other government incentives can attract global investment and foster technological self-reliance in the semiconductor sector.
Evaluate the challenges and opportunities for India to become a significant player in the global semiconductor supply chain.
Examine the broader impact of a thriving semiconductor industry on job creation, innovation, and digital transformation in India.
articles.imageDescription: A close-up of a silicon wafer with intricate circuit patterns.
articles.trivia: A single modern smartphone can contain hundreds of different semiconductor chips.

articles.headline: Animal Welfare Board of India Releases Annual Report, Highlights Gaps in Implementation of Animal Protection Laws
articles.explainer:
• The Animal Welfare Board of India (AWBI) released its annual report, detailing the state of animal welfare and protection across the country.
• The report highlighted significant gaps in the effective implementation of existing animal protection laws, particularly the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals (PCA) Act, 1960.
• It called for stronger enforcement mechanisms, increased public awareness, and greater coordination among state authorities and animal welfare organizations.
• The report also presented data on animal cruelty cases, rescue operations, and sterilization programs, noting regional disparities.
• Recommendations included amendments to the PCA Act to increase penalties and the establishment of dedicated animal protection units in police forces.
gsSubTopics: Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes; mechanisms, laws, institutions and Bodies constituted for the protection and betterment of these vulnerable sections.
tags: Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Act
keyTerms: Prevention of Cruelty to Animals (PCA) Act, 1960
prelimsPoints: Role and functions of Animal Welfare Board of India (AWBI) as a statutory advisory body.
Key provisions of the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals (PCA) Act, 1960.
Constitutional provisions related to animal protection (Article 48A, Article 51A(g)).
Different types of animal cruelty.
mainsPoints: Discuss the ethical and legal dimensions of animal welfare in India and the challenges in implementing animal protection laws.
Analyze the recommendations of the AWBI report and their potential impact on improving animal welfare standards.
Evaluate the role of civil society organizations and government bodies in advocating for and enforcing animal rights.
Examine the need for stronger penalties and dedicated enforcement units to curb animal cruelty and promote responsible pet ownership.
articles.imageDescription: A silhouette of various animals (dog, cow, bird) against a backdrop of a protective hand.
articles.trivia: The Animal Welfare Board of India was established in 1962 under Section 4 of The Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Act, 1960.

articles.headline: Startup India Seed Fund Scheme Extended, Focus on Deep Tech and Rural Innovation
articles.explainer:
• The Department for Promotion of Industry and Internal Trade (DPIIT) announced an extension of the Startup India Seed Fund Scheme (SISFS) for another three years.
• The extended scheme will place a greater emphasis on supporting startups in deep technology, artificial intelligence, and rural innovation.
• Funding will be channeled through incubators to promising startups for proof of concept, prototype development, product trials, and market-entry.
• The initiative aims to foster entrepreneurship, create jobs, and build a robust innovation ecosystem across all regions of India.
• This extension is expected to provide crucial early-stage capital to startups that often struggle to secure funding.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
Inclusive growth and issues arising from it.
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life; indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
prelimsPoints: Objectives of Startup India initiative.
Features of the Startup India Seed Fund Scheme.
Role of incubators in the startup ecosystem.
Definition of 'deep technology' and 'rural innovation'.
Government support mechanisms for startups (e.g., tax benefits, IPR facilitation).
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of government-backed seed funding schemes in fostering entrepreneurship and innovation in India, particularly for early-stage startups.
Analyze how the extended Startup India Seed Fund Scheme, with its focus on deep tech and rural innovation, can contribute to inclusive growth and technological advancement.
Evaluate the challenges faced by startups in securing early-stage funding and how such schemes help address these gaps.
Suggest measures to further strengthen India's startup ecosystem, focusing on mentorship, market access, and regulatory support.
articles.imageDescription: A stylized graphic showing a seedling growing out of a circuit board, representing tech innovation.
articles.trivia: India has the third-largest startup ecosystem in the world, after the US and China.

articles.headline: India Sets Ambitious New Target of 500 GW Renewable Energy Capacity by 2030
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of New and Renewable Energy announced an ambitious new target to achieve 500 GW of renewable energy capacity by 2030, raising the previous commitment.
• This revised target underscores India's commitment to climate action and transitioning towards a low-carbon economy.
• The focus will be on accelerated deployment of solar and wind energy projects, along with increased investment in energy storage solutions.
• The move is expected to drive massive investments in the renewable energy sector, create green jobs, and enhance energy security.
• Challenges include land acquisition, grid integration, and financing, which the government plans to address through policy reforms and innovative mechanisms.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
prelimsPoints: India's current renewable energy capacity and previous targets.
Components of renewable energy (solar, wind, hydro, biomass).
Role of International Solar Alliance (ISA).
Challenges in renewable energy integration (grid stability, storage).
Government schemes for promoting renewable energy (e.g., PM-KUSUM).
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of India's ambitious new renewable energy target for its climate commitments, energy security, and economic growth.
Analyze the opportunities and challenges in achieving 500 GW of renewable energy capacity by 2030, focusing on technological, financial, and infrastructural aspects.
Evaluate the role of policy reforms, international cooperation, and private sector investment in accelerating India's transition to a low-carbon economy.
Examine the broader implications of renewable energy expansion for job creation, rural development, and reducing dependence on fossil fuels.
articles.imageDescription: A vast solar farm stretching to the horizon under a bright sun.
articles.trivia: India is the world's third-largest energy consuming country and has one of the fastest-growing renewable energy programs.

articles.headline: Ministry of Tourism to Revise National Tourism Policy for Sustainable and Inclusive Growth
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Tourism announced plans to revise the National Tourism Policy to promote sustainable, responsible, and inclusive tourism in India.
• The updated policy will focus on enhancing infrastructure, promoting niche tourism products (e.g., eco-tourism, adventure tourism, spiritual tourism), and leveraging digital platforms.
• Emphasis will be placed on community participation, skill development in the hospitality sector, and ensuring the safety and security of tourists.
• The revision aims to boost India's global competitiveness as a tourist destination and contribute significantly to economic growth and employment.
• Public and private stakeholders will be consulted to develop a comprehensive policy framework that addresses contemporary challenges and opportunities.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
prelimsPoints: Importance of tourism for India's economy and employment.
Different types of tourism (eco-tourism, medical tourism, spiritual tourism).
Government initiatives for tourism promotion (e.g., Swadesh Darshan, Prasad scheme).
Concept of sustainable and responsible tourism.
Role of Ministry of Tourism.
mainsPoints: Discuss the potential of the tourism sector to drive economic growth and employment in India, particularly in rural and remote areas.
Analyze the need for a revised National Tourism Policy to address contemporary challenges and promote sustainable and inclusive tourism practices.
Evaluate how infrastructural development, promotion of niche tourism, and digital integration can enhance India's global competitiveness as a tourist destination.
Suggest measures to ensure that tourism development is balanced with environmental conservation and cultural preservation.
articles.imageDescription: A collage of diverse Indian tourist attractions, from beaches to mountains and historical sites.
articles.trivia: The 'Incredible India' campaign is a major international marketing campaign by the Government of India to promote tourism.

articles.headline: Indian AI Startup Launches 'BharatGPT', a Multilingual Large Language Model
articles.explainer:
• An Indian AI startup, 'Vani AI', today unveiled 'BharatGPT', an advanced multilingual Large Language Model (LLM) designed specifically for Indian languages.
• BharatGPT supports over 22 scheduled Indian languages, aiming to democratize access to AI technologies and cater to India's linguistic diversity.
• The model is trained on vast datasets of Indian texts and cultural contexts, promising more accurate and culturally nuanced responses.
• This development is a significant step towards indigenous AI innovation, reducing dependence on foreign models and fostering digital inclusivity.
• Applications are envisioned across education, e-governance, customer service, and content creation in regional languages.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life; indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
Awareness in the fields of IT, Space, Computers, robotics, nano-technology, bio-technology and issues relating to intellectual property rights.
prelimsPoints: Definition of Large Language Model (LLM) and Artificial Intelligence (AI).
Significance of multilingual AI for India.
Scheduled languages of India (8th Schedule of the Constitution).
Applications of AI in various sectors (education, governance, healthcare).
Challenges in developing AI for diverse linguistic contexts.
mainsPoints: Discuss the transformative potential of indigenous multilingual Large Language Models like 'BharatGPT' for digital inclusivity and economic development in India.
Analyze the technological challenges and opportunities in building AI models that cater to India's vast linguistic and cultural diversity.
Evaluate the implications of such innovations for reducing digital divide, enhancing e-governance, and fostering local content creation.
Examine the ethical considerations and policy frameworks required to ensure responsible development and deployment of AI in India.
articles.imageDescription: A stylized brain icon composed of Indian language scripts, representing multilingual AI.
articles.trivia: India has 22 official languages and hundreds of other recognized languages and dialects, making it one of the most linguistically diverse countries.

highlights: Supreme Court stays UGC Equity Regulations, 2026, citing potential for misuse by specific groups, impacting higher education.
India and Japan conclude 10th Strategic Dialogue, focusing on Indo-Pacific security, economic cooperation, and critical minerals.
Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission expands to cover all Primary Health Centers by year-end 2026, boosting digital health access.
ISRO prepares for Mars Orbiter Mission-2 (Mangalyaan-2) launch by end of 2026, aiming for advanced Martian studies.
Government unveils National Cyber Security Framework 2.0 and enhances incentives for semiconductor manufacturing, strengthening digital and economic security.

prelimsMocks.question:
With reference to the recent Supreme Court stay on UGC Equity Regulations, 2026, consider the following statements:
1. The stay is an interim order, and the Supreme Court has sought responses from the UGC and Ministry of Education.
2. The regulations aimed to promote equitable access and representation in higher education institutions.
3. The Supreme Court's action falls under its advisory jurisdiction.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is correct. The Supreme Court issued an interim stay and sought responses. Statement 2 is correct, as the regulations aimed to promote equity. Statement 3 is incorrect. The Supreme Court's action in staying a regulation falls under its original or appellate jurisdiction, specifically related to judicial review, not its advisory jurisdiction (Article 143).

prelimsMocks.question: The 'BharatNet' project, recently in news, refers to:
options: (a) A government initiative for rural optical fiber broadband connectivity.
(b) India's first privately developed satellite constellation for broadband internet.
(c) A national digital payment platform for cross-border transactions.
(d) A program for indigenously developed 5G telecom equipment manufacturing.
prelimsMocks.answer: (b) India's first privately developed satellite constellation for broadband internet.
prelimsMocks.explanation: While 'BharatNet' is also a government project for optical fiber, in the context of the news, it refers to India's first privately developed satellite constellation for broadband internet, achieving full operational capability.

prelimsMocks.question:
Consider the following statements regarding India's National Policy on Climate-Resilient Agriculture (NPCRA):
1. It focuses on integrating climate change adaptation and mitigation strategies into agricultural practices.
2. The policy promotes drought-resistant crop varieties and efficient water management techniques.
3. It is primarily focused on organic farming and reducing chemical inputs.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statements 1 and 2 are correct as per the news, highlighting adaptation, mitigation, drought-resistant crops, and water management. Statement 3 is incorrect as the primary focus mentioned is broader climate resilience, not exclusively organic farming, although sustainable practices might include aspects of organic farming.

prelimsMocks.question: Which of the following bodies is responsible for implementing the Namami Gange Mission?
options: (c) Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change
prelimsMocks.explanation: The National Mission for Clean Ganga (NMCG) is the implementing agency for the Namami Gange program, functioning under the Ministry of Jal Shakti.

prelimsMocks.question:
With reference to the National Cyber Security Framework 2.0, consider the following features:
1. Proactive and adaptive approach to cyber defense.
2. Mandatory incident reporting for critical sectors.
3. Exclusive focus on government agencies' cybersecurity.
4. Promotion of indigenous cybersecurity capabilities.
Which of the features given above are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statements 1, 2, and 4 are correct as per the news, emphasizing proactive defense, mandatory reporting, and indigenous capabilities. Statement 3 is incorrect; the framework focuses on critical infrastructure and public-private partnerships, not exclusively government agencies.

mainsMocks.question: The Supreme Court's stay on UGC Equity Regulations, 2026 highlights the complex interplay between policy-making, social justice, and judicial review. Critically examine the implications of such judicial interventions on governance and the pursuit of equitable development in India.
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS2: Indian Constitution; Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors; Structure, organization and functioning of the Executive and the Judiciary.
mainsMocks.approach: Introduce the SC's decision and the context of equity regulations. Discuss the constitutional basis for judicial review and affirmative action. Analyze the implications for policy implementation, institutional autonomy, and social justice. Evaluate the balance between judicial oversight and legislative/executive functions. Conclude on the necessity of checks and balances for equitable governance.

mainsMocks.question: India's ambitious new target of 500 GW renewable energy capacity by 2030 underscores its commitment to climate action and energy security. Discuss the opportunities and challenges in achieving this target and suggest measures for a smooth and sustainable energy transition.
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS3: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation; Infrastructure: Energy; Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
mainsMocks.approach: Briefly state India's new target and its significance. Detail the opportunities (economic growth, job creation, reduced imports, climate benefits). Enumerate the challenges (land acquisition, grid integration, financing, technology, storage). Suggest measures including policy reforms, technological innovation, international cooperation, and private sector investment. Conclude on the importance of a holistic approach for sustainable energy transition.

mainsMocks.question: The launch of 'National Cyber Security Framework 2.0' and enhanced incentives for semiconductor manufacturing are critical steps towards India's digital and economic sovereignty. Analyze how these initiatives contribute to 'Atmanirbhar Bharat' and address contemporary challenges in technology and security.
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS3: Basics of cyber security; Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life; Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
mainsMocks.approach: Introduce both initiatives and their relevance. For cybersecurity, discuss evolving threats and how the framework strengthens India's digital resilience and protects critical infrastructure. For semiconductors, explain their strategic importance and how incentives foster indigenous manufacturing, reducing import dependence. Connect both to 'Atmanirbhar Bharat' (self-reliant India). Conclude on the synergistic effect of these policies in building a secure and technologically advanced nation.

quickBytes.text: The UGC is a statutory body established in 1956 under the UGC Act, 1956.
Article 14 of the Indian Constitution guarantees equality before law and equal protection of laws.
The Indo-Pacific region is considered the geopolitical center of the 21st century.
Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission aims to create a national digital health ecosystem.
Mangalyaan-1 (MOM) was launched by ISRO in 2013 using a PSLV-C25 rocket.
GST is a consumption-based tax levied on the supply of goods and services.
The Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Act was enacted in 1960.
LEO satellites are typically used for low-latency communication due to their proximity to Earth.
Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) holds producers accountable for end-of-life management of products.
India's 8th Schedule of the Constitution lists 22 official languages.

GS1.headline: New National Heritage Conservation Policy to Protect Intangible Cultural Heritage
GS1.explainer:
• The Ministry of Culture launched the 'National Policy for the Conservation of Intangible Cultural Heritage (ICH)', a first of its kind in India.
• The policy aims to identify, document, preserve, and promote India's rich intangible cultural heritage, including oral traditions, performing arts, social practices, rituals, festive events, and traditional craftsmanship.
• It will establish a national registry for ICH, provide funding for community-led conservation projects, and integrate ICH into educational curricula.
• The initiative seeks to empower local communities and traditional practitioners who are custodians of this heritage, ensuring its transmission to future generations.
• This policy is crucial for safeguarding India's diverse cultural identity and promoting cultural tourism.
gsSubTopics: Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature and Architecture from ancient to modern times.
Salient features of Indian Society, Diversity of India.
prelimsPoints: Definition of Intangible Cultural Heritage (ICH).
Examples of ICH recognized by UNESCO from India (e.g., Yoga, Kumbh Mela).
Key features of the new National Policy.
Role of Ministry of Culture.
Difference between tangible and intangible heritage.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of India's intangible cultural heritage and the necessity of a dedicated policy for its conservation and promotion.
Analyze the challenges involved in identifying, documenting, and safeguarding ICH, particularly in a diverse country like India.
Evaluate how community participation and integration into education can ensure the vitality and transmission of traditional knowledge and practices.
Examine the potential of ICH for cultural tourism and its role in strengthening national identity and international cultural diplomacy.
GS1.imageDescription: An image depicting various forms of Indian performing arts and traditional crafts.
GS1.trivia: UNESCO's 2003 Convention for the Safeguarding of the Intangible Cultural Heritage aims to protect such treasures worldwide.

GS2.headline: Supreme Court Stays UGC Equity Regulations, 2026 Amidst Misuse Concerns
GS2.explainer:
• The Supreme Court of India today issued an interim stay on the implementation of the University Grants Commission (UGC) Equity Regulations, 2026.
• The Court cited concerns regarding the potential for misuse of provisions by 'specific groups', following several petitions challenging the regulations' framework.
• The regulations aimed to promote equitable access and representation in higher education institutions, potentially through affirmative action policies or specific funding allocations.
• This decision is expected to have significant implications for admissions processes, faculty recruitment, and institutional autonomy across universities and colleges nationwide.
• The apex court has sought detailed responses from the UGC and the Ministry of Education, scheduling the next hearing in April.
gsSubTopics: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure.
Functions and responsibilities of the Union and the States, issues and challenges pertaining to the federal structure, devolution of powers and finances up to local levels and challenges therein.
Structure, organization and functioning of the Executive and the Judiciary Ministries and Departments of the Government; pressure groups and formal/informal associations and their role in the Polity.
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
prelimsPoints: Supreme Court's power of judicial review (Article 13, 32, 226).
Role and functions of University Grants Commission (UGC) as a statutory body.
Constitutional provisions related to equality and affirmative action (Articles 14, 15, 16).
Difference between interim stay and permanent injunction.
mainsPoints: Examine the balance between legislative intent (UGC Regulations) and judicial oversight (SC stay) in policy implementation.
Discuss the constitutional validity and socio-economic implications of affirmative action policies in higher education.
Analyze the challenges of implementing equity regulations and preventing their misuse, considering diverse societal interests.
Evaluate the role of the judiciary in safeguarding fundamental rights and ensuring fair implementation of government policies.
GS2.imageDescription: Illustration of the Supreme Court of India building with legal documents in the foreground.
GS2.trivia: The first UGC was established in 1945 to oversee the three Central Universities of Aligarh, Banaras, and Delhi, and its scope was extended in 1947 to cover all Indian universities.

GS2.headline: India-Japan 10th Strategic Dialogue Concludes, Focus on Indo-Pacific Security and Economic Cooperation
GS2.explainer:
• The 10th India-Japan Strategic Dialogue concluded in New Delhi, reaffirming commitment to a 'Special Strategic and Global Partnership'.
• Discussions focused on enhancing cooperation in the Indo-Pacific region, counter-terrorism, and defense exchanges.
• Both nations emphasized strengthening supply chain resilience and accelerating investments in infrastructure and digital technologies.
• Key agreements were reached on joint projects in renewable energy and critical minerals extraction, leveraging Japan's technology and India's growing market.
• The dialogue also addressed regional challenges, including maritime security and stability in East Asia.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighborhood- relations.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
prelimsPoints: Key pillars of India-Japan relations (security, economy, technology).
Significance of the Indo-Pacific region for both countries.
Role of QUAD grouping and other multilateral forums involving India and Japan.
Major economic and strategic projects between India and Japan (e.g., bullet train, infrastructure).
mainsPoints: Analyze the evolving strategic partnership between India and Japan in the context of changing geopolitical dynamics in the Indo-Pacific.
Discuss how economic cooperation, particularly in critical sectors like renewable energy and digital tech, strengthens India's strategic autonomy.
Evaluate the challenges and opportunities for India-Japan collaboration in maintaining regional security and stability.
Examine the role of bilateral dialogues in shaping India's foreign policy objectives and addressing global challenges.
GS2.imageDescription: Flags of India and Japan displayed during a diplomatic meeting.
GS2.trivia: Japan is one of the few countries with which India has a 2+2 Ministerial Dialogue (Foreign and Defence Ministers).

GS2.headline: Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission Expands to Cover All Primary Health Centers by Year-End
GS2.explainer:
• The Union Ministry of Health and Family Welfare announced an ambitious plan to integrate all Primary Health Centers (PHCs) and Community Health Centers (CHCs) into the Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission (ABDM) by the end of 2026.
• This expansion aims to create a seamless digital health ecosystem, enabling electronic health records (EHRs) for all citizens and improving access to healthcare services.
• Training programs for healthcare workers in rural areas will be scaled up to ensure effective adoption of digital tools and platforms.
• The initiative is expected to enhance data-driven policymaking, reduce healthcare disparities, and streamline patient management across public health facilities.
• Challenges related to digital literacy, internet connectivity in remote areas, and data privacy were acknowledged, with mitigation strategies being developed.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.

Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes; mechanisms, laws, institutions and Bodies constituted for the protection and betterment of these vulnerable sections.
prelimsPoints: Objectives and components of Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission.
Role of PHCs and CHCs in India's healthcare delivery system.
Significance of Health ID and its features.
Challenges in digitalizing healthcare in India (connectivity, literacy, privacy).
mainsPoints: Discuss the potential of the Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission to revolutionize healthcare delivery in India, particularly in rural areas.
Analyze the socio-economic benefits and challenges associated with the widespread adoption of digital health records.
Critically evaluate the government's strategy for expanding ABDM, considering issues of data privacy, digital divide, and infrastructure.
Suggest measures to ensure inclusive and equitable access to digital healthcare services for all sections of society.
GS2.imageDescription: A graphic depicting the flow of digital health records between various healthcare providers and patients.
GS2.trivia: ABDM aims to create a seamless online platform through the provision of a wide range of data, information and infrastructure services, leveraging open, interoperable, standards-based digital systems.

GS2.headline: India Initiates Further Review of Indus Water Treaty Provisions Amidst Persistent Disputes
GS2.explainer:
• India has commenced a detailed review of certain provisions of the Indus Water Treaty (IWT) with Pakistan, citing concerns over Pakistan's objections to hydroelectric projects.
• The review aims to explore mechanisms for optimal utilization of India's share of water from the Western Rivers, as per the treaty's mandate.
• This move comes after several rounds of failed bilateral talks and arbitration efforts at international forums regarding projects like Kishenganga and Ratle.
• India reiterates its commitment to the IWT but emphasizes the need for a 'fair and equitable' interpretation that recognizes its developmental needs.
• Experts suggest this could lead to increased pressure on Pakistan to engage constructively or escalate diplomatic tensions.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighborhood- relations.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
prelimsPoints: Year of signing and key provisions of the Indus Water Treaty.
Which rivers are allocated to India and Pakistan under IWT.
Role of the Permanent Indus Commission.
Key hydroelectric projects causing disputes (Kishenganga, Ratle).
mainsPoints: Discuss the historical context and strategic importance of the Indus Water Treaty for both India and Pakistan.
Analyze the reasons behind India's decision to review IWT provisions and its potential implications for bilateral relations.
Evaluate the challenges and opportunities for peaceful resolution of water disputes between riparian states, citing international best practices.
Examine how water resources can be a source of both conflict and cooperation in South Asia.
GS2.imageDescription: Map showing the Indus River System and the allocation of rivers under the IWT.
GS2.trivia: The Indus Waters Treaty was brokered by the World Bank and signed in 1960 by Prime Minister Jawaharlal Nehru and President Ayub Khan.

GS2.headline: India-Africa Free Trade Agreement Negotiations Advance, Boosting South-South Cooperation
GS2.explainer:
• India and a bloc of African nations have made significant progress in negotiations for a comprehensive Free Trade Agreement (FTA).
• The proposed FTA aims to reduce tariffs, streamline customs procedures, and enhance market access for goods and services between India and participating African countries.
• This initiative is part of India's broader strategy to strengthen South-South cooperation and diversify its trade partnerships.
• Key sectors targeted for increased trade and investment include pharmaceuticals, automotive, agriculture, and digital services.
• The agreement is expected to foster economic growth, create employment opportunities, and build stronger diplomatic ties.
gsSubTopics: Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
prelimsPoints: Definition and objectives of a Free Trade Agreement (FTA).
Key sectors of India-Africa trade.
Concept of South-South Cooperation.
India's existing trade agreements with other regions/countries.
mainsPoints: Discuss the strategic importance of an India-Africa Free Trade Agreement in strengthening economic ties and promoting South-South cooperation.
Analyze the potential benefits and challenges for India and African nations in implementing a comprehensive FTA.
Evaluate how such agreements contribute to India's foreign policy objectives and its role as a global economic power.
Examine the impact of trade liberalization on developing economies, considering issues of industrial development and local livelihoods.
GS2.imageDescription: Flags of India and various African nations converging, symbolizing trade partnership.
GS2.trivia: India is Africa's fourth-largest trading partner, and bilateral trade has been growing steadily over the past two decades.

GS2.headline: Animal Welfare Board of India Releases Annual Report, Highlights Gaps in Implementation of Animal Protection Laws
GS2.explainer:
• The Animal Welfare Board of India (AWBI) released its annual report, detailing the state of animal welfare and protection across the country.
• The report highlighted significant gaps in the effective implementation of existing animal protection laws, particularly the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals (PCA) Act, 1960.
• It called for stronger enforcement mechanisms, increased public awareness, and greater coordination among state authorities and animal welfare organizations.
• The report also presented data on animal cruelty cases, rescue operations, and sterilization programs, noting regional disparities.
• Recommendations included amendments to the PCA Act to increase penalties and the establishment of dedicated animal protection units in police forces.
gsSubTopics: Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes; mechanisms, laws, institutions and Bodies constituted for the protection and betterment of these vulnerable sections.
tags: Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Act
keyTerms: Prevention of Cruelty to Animals (PCA) Act, 1960
prelimsPoints: Role and functions of Animal Welfare Board of India (AWBI) as a statutory advisory body.
Key provisions of the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals (PCA) Act, 1960.
Constitutional provisions related to animal protection (Article 48A, Article 51A(g)).
Different types of animal cruelty.
mainsPoints: Discuss the ethical and legal dimensions of animal welfare in India and the challenges in implementing animal protection laws.
Analyze the recommendations of the AWBI report and their potential impact on improving animal welfare standards.
Evaluate the role of civil society organizations and government bodies in advocating for and enforcing animal rights.
Examine the need for stronger penalties and dedicated enforcement units to curb animal cruelty and promote responsible pet ownership.
GS2.imageDescription: A silhouette of various animals (dog, cow, bird) against a backdrop of a protective hand.
GS2.trivia: The Animal Welfare Board of India was established in 1962 under Section 4 of The Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Act, 1960.

GS3.headline: ISRO Prepares for Mars Orbiter Mission-2 Launch by End of 2026
GS3.explainer:
• The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) confirmed its readiness to launch the Mars Orbiter Mission-2 (MOM-2), also known as Mangalyaan-2, by the end of 2026.
• MOM-2 aims for a more advanced study of the Martian surface, atmosphere, and subsurface, carrying a suite of new scientific instruments.
• This mission builds upon the success of Mangalyaan-1, which made India the first Asian nation to reach Mars orbit and the first country to do so in its maiden attempt.
• Key objectives include understanding the evolution of Mars, searching for signs of past or present life, and analyzing Martian geology and mineralogy.
• The mission will likely involve an orbiter, and potentially a lander/rover component, though specifics are still under wraps.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
Awareness in the fields of Space.
prelimsPoints: First Mars mission (MOM-1) and its achievements.
Objectives of MOM-2 (Mangalyaan-2).
Scientific instruments typically carried on planetary missions.
Key aspects of Mars' environment (atmosphere, geology, water history).
Launch vehicles used by ISRO for deep space missions (e.g., PSLV, GSLV).
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of India's Mars missions for its space program, scientific community, and international standing.
Analyze the scientific objectives of MOM-2 and how they contribute to our understanding of planetary evolution and habitability.
Evaluate the technological challenges involved in interplanetary missions and ISRO's capabilities in overcoming them.
Examine the broader implications of space exploration for national development, technological innovation, and international cooperation.
GS3.imageDescription: Artistic rendition of an Indian satellite orbiting Mars with its scientific instruments deployed.
GS3.trivia: India's Mangalyaan (MOM-1) mission was the cheapest Mars mission ever, costing only about $74 million.

GS3.headline: GST Collections Hit Record High in January 2026, Signifying Robust Economic Growth
GS3.explainer:
• Goods and Services Tax (GST) collections for January 2026 (for transactions in December 2025) reached an all-time high, surpassing previous records.
• The Ministry of Finance attributed the surge to sustained economic recovery, increased compliance, and festive demand.
• This robust collection indicates a healthy growth trajectory for the Indian economy, reflecting strong manufacturing and service sector activity.
• The higher tax revenue provides the government with greater fiscal space for infrastructure spending and social welfare programs.
• Experts suggest that improved tax administration and technology-driven compliance measures have also played a crucial role in enhancing collections.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
Government Budgeting.
Inclusive growth and issues arising from it.
prelimsPoints: Components of GST (CGST, SGST, IGST, UTGST).
Role of GST Council and its composition.
Impact of GST on Indian economy (pros and cons).
Relationship between tax collection and economic indicators (GDP growth, consumption).
mainsPoints: Analyze the factors contributing to the record GST collections and their implications for India's fiscal health and economic stability.
Discuss how a robust tax collection system can support government initiatives for infrastructure development and social welfare.
Evaluate the effectiveness of GST in streamlining indirect taxation and promoting ease of doing business in India.
Examine the challenges that remain in further improving GST compliance and expanding the tax base.
GS3.imageDescription: A bar graph showing increasing GST collection trends over months.
GS3.trivia: GST was implemented in India on 1 July 2017, replacing multiple cascading taxes levied by the central and state governments.

GS3.headline: New National Policy on Climate-Resilient Agriculture Launched to Bolster Food Security
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of Agriculture and Farmers' Welfare unveiled a comprehensive National Policy on Climate-Resilient Agriculture (NPCRA).
• The policy aims to integrate climate change adaptation and mitigation strategies into agricultural practices across the country.
• Key focus areas include promoting drought-resistant crop varieties, efficient water management techniques, and sustainable land use practices.
• NPCRA will facilitate research and development in climate-smart agriculture, provide financial incentives to farmers, and enhance capacity building.
• The initiative is crucial for ensuring food security and farmers' livelihoods in the face of increasing climate variability and extreme weather events.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Disaster and disaster management.

Major crops cropping patterns in various parts of the country, different types of irrigation and irrigation systems storage, transport and marketing of agricultural produce and issues and related constraints; e-technology in the aid of farmers.

Issues related to direct and indirect farm subsidies and minimum support prices; Public Distribution System objectives, functioning, limitations, revamping; issues of buffer stocks and food security; Technology missions; economics of animal-rearing.
prelimsPoints: Definition of climate-resilient agriculture.
Key components of the new NPCRA policy.
Examples of climate-smart agricultural practices.
Government schemes related to agricultural sustainability (e.g., PM Fasal Bima Yojana, Paramparagat Krishi Vikas Yojana).
mainsPoints: Discuss the imperative of adopting climate-resilient agricultural practices in India to safeguard food security and farmers' income.
Analyze the key features and potential impact of the National Policy on Climate-Resilient Agriculture on Indian farming.
Evaluate the challenges in implementing such a policy, including technological adoption, farmer awareness, and financial support.
Suggest a multi-stakeholder approach for promoting sustainable and climate-resilient agriculture across different agro-climatic zones in India.
GS3.imageDescription: A farmer working in a field with drought-resistant crops under a bright sun.
GS3.trivia: India is highly vulnerable to climate change due to its large population dependent on agriculture and its diverse agro-climatic zones.

GS3.headline: Government Unveils New National Cyber Security Framework 2.0 to Combat Evolving Threats
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY) launched the National Cyber Security Framework 2.0, updating the previous policy from 2013.
• The new framework emphasizes a proactive and adaptive approach to cyber defense, focusing on critical infrastructure protection, threat intelligence sharing, and public-private partnerships.
• It introduces stricter data governance norms, mandatory incident reporting for critical sectors, and enhanced training for cybersecurity professionals.
• The policy aims to foster indigenous cybersecurity capabilities and promote research and development in emerging technologies like AI and quantum computing for security applications.
• This move is crucial for safeguarding India's digital economy and protecting citizens from increasingly sophisticated cyberattacks.
gsSubTopics: Challenges to internal security through communication networks, role of media and social networking sites in internal security challenges, basics of cyber security; money-laundering and its prevention.
Security challenges and their management in border areas; linkages of organized crime with terrorism.
prelimsPoints: Key objectives of National Cyber Security Framework 2.0.
Role of CERT-In (Indian Computer Emergency Response Team).
Concept of Critical Information Infrastructure (CII).
Types of cyber threats (ransomware, phishing, DDoS).
Differences from previous cybersecurity policies.
mainsPoints: Discuss the evolving landscape of cyber threats and the necessity for India to adopt a robust and adaptive national cybersecurity framework.
Analyze the key provisions of the National Cyber Security Framework 2.0 and its potential impact on critical sectors and data protection.
Evaluate the challenges in implementing a comprehensive cybersecurity policy, including technological gaps, human resource shortages, and international cooperation.
Suggest measures to strengthen India's cyber resilience, emphasizing public awareness, private sector involvement, and indigenous innovation.
GS3.imageDescription: A stylized digital network grid with a lock icon, representing cybersecurity.
GS3.trivia: India ranks among the top countries globally in terms of internet users, making it a significant target for cyberattacks.

GS3.headline: Namami Gange Mission Enters Phase III, Focus on Tributaries and Sustainable River Basin Management
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of Jal Shakti announced the commencement of Phase III of the Namami Gange Mission, expanding its scope beyond the main stem of the Ganga.
• This new phase will primarily focus on rejuvenating the tributaries of the Ganga, including Yamuna, Ramganga, and Gomti, addressing pollution hotspots.
• Emphasis will be laid on decentralized wastewater treatment, promoting circular economy principles, and community participation in river conservation.
• Integrated river basin management plans will be developed for all major tributaries to ensure holistic ecological restoration.
• The initiative aims to achieve 'Nirmal' (clean) and 'Aviral' (uninterrupted flow) Ganga, along with its entire river system.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Disaster and disaster management.
prelimsPoints: Objectives of Namami Gange Mission.
Key focus areas of Phase III (tributaries).
Major tributaries of the Ganga river system.
Concept of 'Nirmal Ganga' and 'Aviral Ganga'.
Types of pollution affecting river systems.
mainsPoints: Discuss the evolution and achievements of the Namami Gange Mission and the necessity of extending its focus to tributaries in Phase III.
Analyze the challenges involved in comprehensive river basin management, considering diverse sources of pollution and stakeholder interests.
Evaluate the role of decentralized wastewater treatment and community participation in achieving sustainable river rejuvenation.
Suggest innovative approaches and policy reforms to enhance the effectiveness of river conservation efforts in India.
GS3.imageDescription: A graphic showing the Ganga river system with its major tributaries highlighted.
GS3.trivia: The Ganga river basin is the largest in India, covering over 25% of the country's land area and supporting about 40% of its population.
